column2: Welcome to Flightdeck, a factory that turns a rough idea into working software. You drop in a feature request, and a team of A-I workers plans it, builds it, tests it, and hands you a live preview to try. Let's walk the floor together.

Normally, shipping a feature is slow and done by hand. One person plans, another codes, someone else tests, and a mistake at any handoff can quietly slip through. Flightdeck automates the whole line, and inspects every single step.

Think of a car factory. Parts go in one end, and a finished, inspected car rolls out the other. Flightdeck is that, but for software. Each station does one job, and a quality checkpoint signs off before the work is allowed to move on.

Here's the real, live pipeline. A request arrives, and the first worker, an OpenAI step, reads it and writes a concrete plan. Then the very first checkpoint asks the obvious question, did we actually get a plan, before anyone writes a line of code.

Now the star of the show. An OpenAI coding agent opens the project and writes the real code, just like a developer would. The moment it finishes, the next checkpoint asks, does it even build? Code that won't build never advances. This is the hardest part to get right.

Building isn't the same as working. So the next station runs the project's tests to prove the feature actually behaves correctly. Only a change that genuinely passes is allowed to continue toward a live preview.

Now Render takes the stage. It puts the tested version online as a temporary, clickable copy, with its own web address, and the factory actually visits it to confirm it's alive. Render is where every result becomes something a human can click and try.

And here's the payoff. A pull request opens automatically, with a green button straight to the live preview. This was proven on five real requests, with one going all the way to a shipped change, and the whole pipeline running live in the cloud. That's the factory.

This is the planning station. An OpenAI step reads the vague request and turns it into a concrete, buildable plan, naming what to change and what done should look like, so the coder has something exact to follow instead of guessing.

This is the heavy lifting. An OpenAI coding agent opens the real project, writes the actual code changes from the plan, and checks that everything still builds. Real code, written automatically. This is the genuinely hard part, and the heart of the whole idea.

Building isn't working. So this station runs the project's tests against the new code, to prove the feature truly behaves the way it should. It's the literal answer to the question, did the change actually do what it was supposed to?

Here the tested version goes live. The factory puts it online on Render as a temporary, running copy with its own web address, that anyone can open and click. This is the moment the result stops being a description and becomes a real thing you can use.

This is the finish line. The factory opens a pull request, the standard way developers propose a change, with the live preview link commented on it and a plain-English summary inside. It's the tidy package handed to a human to glance over and approve.

This is a checkpoint, and checkpoints are the whole point. After a station finishes, the gate asks one yes-or-no question, did that step really succeed? A yes lets the work move on. A no stops the line right there, so nothing broken is ever passed downstream.

Here's the real headline. Anyone can build an A-I that writes code. The trick is an A-I you can trust to ship. After every station, a checkpoint asks, did that actually work, and only a yes moves forward. That's what makes the output trustworthy.

This is Superplane conducting the line. It runs the stations in the right order, carries each result to the next, and keeps a separate run for every request, so all five issues can move through the factory at once. It always remembers exactly where each one is.

This is Superplane enforcing the checks. After each step it runs a gate, a small yes-or-no test that confirms the previous stage really succeeded. A pass moves on, a fail halts the run. That checkpoint after every stage is the whole spine of the system.

Superplane can also think for itself. Two of the stations are OpenAI steps built right into the canvas, with no extra service to call. One reads the issue and writes the plan, the other reviews the finished work and writes the summary that lands on the pull request.

This is the light preview tier. For a self-contained piece of interface, the factory builds a small static demo of the changed component and hosts it on Render as a static site. There's no backend to keep warm, just an instant public link that never spins down.

This is the heavy preview tier. For a full, end-to-end flow, the factory builds a complete image of the modified app and asks Render to run it as a real web service, then reads back the live address. It's the whole running application, ready to click through.

So what is a pull request? It's simply the polite, standard way a developer proposes a change. Here's exactly what I changed, here's why, please take a look. Nothing touches the real project until a human approves it, so Flightdeck's output drops into a workflow people already know.

And what is a preview environment? It's a temporary, live copy of the feature, on its own web address, completely separate from anything real. Like a test-drive car on the lot, fully working but safe to poke at. It turns trust me, it works, into see for yourself.

The star employee here is an OpenAI coding agent. It takes the plan, opens the real project, writes the code changes, and confirms the build still passes, exactly what a developer does, but automatically. Writing correct code in a real codebase is the genuinely hard part, solved.
